## EXPRESSIVENESS AND APPROXIMATION PROPERTIES OF GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS

## **Floris Geerts**

Department of Computer Science, University of Antwerp, Belgium floris.geerts@uantwerpen.be

## **Juan L. Reutter**

School of Engineering, Pontificia Universidad Cat´olica de Chile, Chile & IMFD, Chile jreutter@ing.puc.cl

## ABSTRACT

Characterizing the separation power of graph neural networks (GNNs) provides an understanding of their limitations for graph learning tasks. Results regarding separation power are, however, usually geared at specific GNN architectures, and tools for understanding arbitrary GNN architectures are generally lacking. We provide an elegant way to easily obtain bounds on the separation power of GNNs in terms of the Weisfeiler-Leman (WL) tests, which have become the yardstick to measure the separation power of GNNs. The crux is to view GNNs as expressions in a procedural tensor language describing the computations in the layers of the GNNs. Then, by a simple analysis of the obtained expressions, in terms of the number of indexes and the nesting depth of summations, bounds on the separation power in terms of the WL-tests readily follow. We use tensor language to define Higher-Order Message-Passing Neural Networks (or _k_ -MPNNs), a natural extension of MPNNs. Furthermore, the tensor language point of view allows for the derivation of universality results for classes of GNNs in a natural way. Our approach provides a toolbox with which GNN architecture designers can analyze the separation power of their GNNs, without needing to know the intricacies of the WL-tests. We also provide insights in what is needed to boost the separation power of GNNs.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) (Merkwirth & Lengauer, 2005; Scarselli et al., 2009) cover many popular deep learning methods for graph learning tasks (see Hamilton (2020) for a recent overview). These methods typically compute vector embeddings of vertices or graphs by relying on the underlying adjacency information. Invariance (for graph embeddings) and equivariance (for vertex embeddings) of GNNs ensure that these methods are oblivious to the precise representation of the graphs.

**Separation power.** Our primary focus is on the separation power of GNN architectures, i.e., on their ability to separate vertices or graphs by means of the computed embeddings. It has become standard to characterize GNN architectures in terms of the separation power of graph algorithms such as color refinement (CR) and _k_ -dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman tests ( _k_ -WL), as initiated in Xu et al. (2019) and Morris et al. (2019). Unfortunately, understanding the separation power of any given GNN architecture requires complex proofs, geared at the specifics of the architecture. We provide a _tensor language-based technique_ to analyze the separation power of general GNNs.

**Tensor languages.** Matrix query languages (Brijder et al., 2019; Geerts et al., 2021b) are defined to assess the expressive power of linear algebra. Balcilar et al. (2021a) observe that, by casting various GNNs into the MATLANG (Brijder et al., 2019) matrix query language, one can use existing separation results (Geerts, 2021) to obtain upper bounds on the separation power of GNNs in terms of 1-WL and 2-WL. In this paper, we considerably extend this approach by defining, and studying, a new general-purpose tensor language specifically designed for modeling GNNs. As in Balcilar et al. (2021a), our focus on tensor languages allows us to obtain new insights about GNN architectures.

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

First, since tensor languages can only define invariant and equivariant graph functions, any GNN that can be cast in our tensor language inherits these desired properties. More importantly, the separation power of our tensor language is as closely related to CR and _k_ -WL as GNNs are. Loosely speaking, _if tensor language expressions use k_ + 1 _indices, then their separation power is bounded by k-_ WL _. Furthermore, if the maximum nesting of summations in the expression is t, then t rounds of k-_ WL _are needed to obtain an upper bound on the separation power._ A similar connection is obtained for CR and a fragment of tensor language that we call “guarded” tensor language.

We thus reduce problem of assessing the separation power of _any_ specific GNN architecture to the problem of specifying it in our tensor language, analyzing the number of indices used and counting their summation depth. This is usually much easier than dealing with intricacies of CR and _k_ -WL, as casting GNNs in our tensor language is often as simple as writing down their layer-based definition. We believe that this provides a nice toolbox for GNN designers to assess the separation power of their architecture. We use this toolbox to recover known results about the separation power of specific GNN architectures such as GINs (Xu et al., 2019), GCNs (Kipf & Welling, 2017), Folklore GNNs (Maron et al., 2019b), _k_ -GNNs (Morris et al., 2019), and several others. We also derive new results: we answer an open problem posed by Maron et al. (2019a) by showing that the separation power of Invariant Graph Networks ( _k_ -IGNs), introduced by Maron et al. (2019b), is bounded by ( _k −_ 1)-WL. In addition, we revisit the analysis by Balcilar et al. (2021b) of ChebNet (Defferrard et al., 2016), and show that CayleyNet (Levie et al., 2019) is bounded by 2-WL.

When writing down GNNs in our tensor language, the less indices needed, the stronger the bounds in terms of _k_ -WL we obtain. After all, ( _k −_ 1)-WL is known to be strictly less separating than _k_ -WL (Otto, 2017). Thus, it is important to minimize the number of indices used in tensor language expressions. We connect this number to the notion of _treewidth_ : expressions of treewidth _k_ can be translated into expressions using _k_ + 1 indices. This corresponds to optimizing expressions, as done in many areas in machine learning, by reordering the summations (a.k.a. variable elimination).

**Approximation and universality.** We also consider the ability of GNNs to approximate general invariant or equivariant graph functions. Once more, instead of focusing on specific architectures, we use our tensor languages to obtain general approximation results, which naturally translate to universality results for GNNs. We show: ( _k_ + 1)-index tensor language expressions suffice to approximate any (invariant/equivariant) graph function whose separating power is bounded by _k_ -WL, and we can further refine this by comparing the number of rounds in _k_ -WL with the summation depth of the expressions. These results provide a finer picture than the one obtained by Azizian & Lelarge (2021). Furthermore, focusing on “guarded” tensor expressions yields a similar universality result for CR, a result that, to our knowledge, was not known before. We also provide the link between approximation results for tensor expressions and GNNs, enabling us to transfer our insights into universality properties of GNNs. As an example, we show that _k_ -IGNs can approximate any graph function that is less separating than ( _k−_ 1)-WL. This case was left open in Azizian & Lelarge (2021).

In summary, we draw new and interesting connections between tensor languages, GNN architectures and classic graph algorithms. We provide a general recipe to bound the separation power of GNNs, optimize them, and understand their approximation power. We show the usefulness of our method by recovering several recent results, as well as new results, some of them left open in previous work.

**Related work.** Separation power has been studied for specific classes of GNNs (Morris et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Maron et al., 2019b; Chen et al., 2019; Morris et al., 2020; Azizian & Lelarge, 2021). A first general result concerns the bounds in terms of CR and 1-WL of Message-Passing Neural Networks (Gilmer et al., 2017; Morris et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019). Balcilar et al. (2021a) use the MATLANG matrix query language to obtain upper bounds on the separation power of various GNNs. MATLANG can only be used to obtain bounds up to 2-WL and is limited to matrices. Our tensor language is more general and flexible and allows for reasoning over the number of indices, treewidth, and summation depth of expressions. These are all crucial for our main results. The tensor language introduced resembles sum-MATLANG (Geerts et al., 2021b), but with the added ability to represent tensors. Neither separation power nor guarded fragments were considered in Geerts et al. (2021b). See Section A in the supplementary material for more details. For universality, Azizian & Lelarge (2021) is closest in spirit. Our approach provides an elegant way to recover and extend their results. Azizian & Lelarge (2021) describe how their work (and hence also ours) encompasses previous works (Keriven & Peyr´e, 2019; Maron et al., 2019c; Chen et al., 2019). Our results use connections between _k_ -WL and logics (Immerman & Lander, 1990; Cai et al., 1992), and CR and

## 3 SPECIFYING GNNS

Many GNNs use linear algebra computations on vectors, matrices or tensors, interleaved with the application of activation functions or MLPs. To understand the separation power of GNNs, we introduce a specification language, TL, for tensor language, that allows us to specify any algebraic computation in a procedural way by explicitly stating how each entry is to be computed. We gauge the separation power of GNNs by specifying them as TL expressions, and syntactically analyzing the components of such TL expressions. This technique gives rise to _Higher-Order Message-Passing Neural Networks_ (or _k_ -MPNNs), a natural extension of MPNNs (Gilmer et al., 2017). For simplicity, we present TL using summation aggregation only but arbitrary aggregation functions on multisets of real values can be used as well (Section C.5 in the supplementary material).

## 4 SEPARATION POWER OF TENSOR LANGUAGES

Our first main results concern the characterization of the separation power of tensor languages in terms of the color refinement and _k_ -dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman algorithms. We provide a finegrained characterization by taking the number of rounds of these algorithms into account. This will allow for measuring the separation power of classes of GNNs in terms of their number of layers.

## 4.2 MAIN RESULTS

We first provide a link between _k_ -WL and tensor language expressions using _k_ + 1 index variables: **Theorem 4.1.** _For each k ≥_ 1 _and any collection_ Ω _of functions, ρ_ 1�vwl _[(] k[∞][)]_ � = _ρ_ 1�TL _k_ +1(Ω)� _._

This theorem gives us new insights: if we wish to understand how a new GNN architecture compares against the _k_ -WL algorithms, all we need to do is to show that such an architecture can be represented in TL _k_ +1(Ω), i.e., is a _k_ -MPNN, an arguably much easier endeavor. As an example of how to use this result, it is well known that triangles can be detected by 2-WL but not by 1-WL. Thus, in order to design GNNs that can detect triangles, layer definitions in TL3 rather than TL2 should be used.
We can do much more, relating the rounds of _k_ -WL to the notion of summation depth of TL(Ω) expressions. We also present present similar results for functions computing graph embeddings.

We also consider “augmented” GNNs, which are combined with a preprocessing step in which higher-order graph information is computed. In the supplementary material (Section D.3) we show how TL encodes the preprocessing step, and how this leads to separation bounds in terms of _k_ -WL, where _k_ depends on the treewidth of the graph information used. ## 7 CONCLUSION

Connecting GNNs and tensor languages allows us to use our analysis of tensor languages to understand the separation and approximation power of GNNs. The number of indices and summation depth needed to represent the layers in GNNs determine their separation power in terms of color refinement and Weisfeiler-Leman tests. The framework of _k_ -MPNNs provides a handy toolbox to understand existing and new GNN architectures, and we demonstrate this by recovering several results about the power of GNNs presented recently in the literature, as well as proving new results.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper addresses an important and timely problem in the GNN literature: providing a unified, architecture-agnostic framework for analyzing the separation power of graph neural networks in terms of Weisfeiler-Leman tests. The central contribution—a tensor language in which GNN computations can be expressed, with separation power directly readable from the number of indices and summation nesting depth—is elegant and potentially high-impact, as it promises to replace bespoke, architecture-specific proofs with a systematic "toolbox" approach. The authors substantiate the framework's utility by recovering many known results (GINs, GCNs, k-GNNs, Folklore GNNs) and deriving genuinely new ones, most notably resolving an open question of Maron et al. (2019a) regarding k-IGNs being bounded by (k−1)-WL, and extending the analysis to universality/approximation results including a CR-based universality result apparently new to the literature. The connection drawn to treewidth and variable elimination is a nice conceptual bridge to optimization practices in ML. That said, the introduction is dense and assumes substantial familiarity with prior work (MATLANG, k-WL variants, IGNs, Folklore GNNs) without building up intuition, which may limit accessibility; a motivating worked example early on would significantly aid readers. The contribution also risks being perceived as incremental over Balcilar et al. (2021a), and the authors should more sharply articulate what fundamentally new expressive capacity their tensor language has beyond MATLANG—beyond simply noting it is "specifically designed for modeling GNNs." Finally, claims about the ease of casting arbitrary GNNs into the language should be tempered or evidenced, as the practical difficulty of such translations (especially for attention-based or more exotic architectures) is a key determinant of the framework's real-world utility.

A careful reader will recognize this as a typo and resolve it trivially, but it is a real (minor) inconsistency between the symbol used in the formula and the one named in prose.

comments.title: Weight matrix dimension conflicts with output feature dimension
comments.quote: _**F** ∈_ R _[n][×][ℓ]_ are vertex features such that _**F** i_ : _∈_ R _[ℓ]_ is the feature vector of vertex _i_ , _σ_ is a non-linear activation function, and _**W** ∈_ R _[ℓ][×][ℓ]_ is a weight matrix.
comments.explanation: The passage states W ∈ R^{ℓ×ℓ}, making F' = σ(A·F·W) also of shape n×ℓ, which is consistent. However, later the text describes ℓ expressions ψ_s(x_1), one for each feature column of F', and the following section exposes indices s ∈ [ℓ] for the output. This is self-consistent (input and output dimensions both ℓ), though somewhat restrictive as a 'typical layer' description (typically W is ℓ×ℓ' with possibly different output dimension). This is a minor simplification rather than an error, since it does not affect the subsequent TL exposition. Not a real issue.

comments.title: Strict inclusion direction for WL hierarchy appears reversed
comments.explanation: I initially parsed this as saying that (k+1)-WL's equivalence relation is strictly contained in k-WL's equivalence relation, which would mean (k+1)-WL has FEWER equivalence classes — i.e., is LESS separating than k-WL. But the standard result (Otto 2017, Cai et al. 1992) is the opposite: (k+1)-WL is strictly MORE separating than k-WL, meaning its equivalence relation is strictly contained (finer partition = smaller equivalence relation as a set of pairs). Wait — actually, more separating means the equivalence relation is SMALLER (fewer pairs are equivalent). So ρ_1(vwl_{k+1}^{(t)}) ⊂ ρ_1(vwl_k^{(t)}) would correctly mean (k+1)-WL distinguishes more pairs than k-WL. That's correct. However, the second inclusion ρ_1(vwl_1^{(t)}) ⊂ ρ_1(cr^{(t)}) says 1-WL's equivalence relation is strictly contained in CR's, meaning 1-WL is strictly MORE separating than CR. The paper's summary states '1-WL (as used here) differs from CR by using non-adjacent vertex information; matters only for vertex embeddings' and Grohe 2021 indeed shows 1-WL (folklore) is at least as strong as CR for vertex embeddings. So this is consistent. The inclusions are actually correct under the 'finer = smaller' convention. On reflection, this is fine — no issue.

comments.title: Guarded fragment equality predicate defined twice with same syntax
comments.quote: In GTL(Ω) only equality predicates **1** _xi_ = _xi_ (constant 1) and **1** _xi_ = _xi_ (constant 0) are allowed
comments.explanation: Initially I was confused because the text says two equality predicates are allowed, both written as '**1**_{x_i = x_i}'. One is supposed to evaluate to constant 1, the other to constant 0. But **1**_{x_i = x_i}$ is always 1 (since x_i always equals itself). The second one should presumably be **1**_{x_i ≠ x_i} (which is constant 0) — this is consistent with the grammar definition earlier where op ∈ {=, ≠}. This looks like an OCR misread of the ≠ symbol in the second predicate, so under the OCR caveat I would not normally flag it. However, the summary context shows ≠ is rendered elsewhere, and the earlier syntax passage also shows '=,' and then a corrupted symbol for ≠. Since the plausible intended reading (one predicate with =, one with ≠) is clear and consistent with the grammar, this is likely an OCR artifact rather than a real author error.

comments.title: Claim that θ is equivalent to θ̃ with free index x_1 used ambiguously
comments.explanation: The example shows reducing treewidth-1 expression θ(x_1) = Σ_{x_2} Σ_{x_3} E(x_1,x_2)·E(x_2,x_3) to a 2-variable expression. In the rewritten form θ̃(x_1) = Σ_{x_2} E(x_1,x_2) · (Σ_{x_1} E(x_2,x_1)), the variable x_1 is reused: it is the free variable of the outer expression but is also the bound variable of the inner summation. This is allowed under standard variable-capture/shadowing conventions for TL (the inner Σ_{x_1} rebinds x_1), and indeed this is exactly the trick that allows reduction from 3 to 2 index variables. However, a careful reader might worry that this reuse is not explicitly licensed by the earlier free-variable definitions. The paper does not explicitly state whether variable shadowing/reuse is permitted. Given that the whole point of Proposition 4.5 hinges on being able to reuse variables this way, an explicit statement that rebinding is allowed (or a rename to a fresh variable) would prevent confusion. This is a minor clarity issue rather than an error.

comments.explanation: The passage repeatedly invokes 'Theorem 4.2' as the main bridge between k-MPNN^(t) and t rounds of k-WL (for eGINs, k-FGNNs, (k+1)-IGNs). However, the paper summary lists Theorems 4.1, 4.3, 4.4 and Proposition 4.5 but no Theorem 4.2. This is likely a theorem that exists in the paper but is simply not reproduced in the summary, so it's a forward/external reference rather than a genuine error. I'm flagging it as minor in case the numbering is actually inconsistent, but a careful reader would most likely find Theorem 4.2 stated in Section 4 between 4.1 and 4.3.

comments.title: Claim that GINs are MPNNs conflicts with summary's strict separation
comments.quote: We consider the Graph Isomorphism Networks (GINs) (Xu et al., 2019) and show that these are MPNNs. To do so, we represent them in GTL(Ω).
comments.explanation: Initial concern: the paper summary states 'MPNNs are strictly weaker than 1-MPNNs: TL_2^(t)(Ω) (1-MPNN) strictly more separating than GTL^(t)(Ω) (MPNN)'. GINs are widely known to match 1-WL/CR. The passage here classifies GINs as MPNNs (guarded, GTL-representable), which is consistent with the CR bound (Theorem 4.3). The GIN aggregation uses only ∑_{u ∈ N_G(v)}, which is guarded summation, so GTL representability is correct. On reflection this is consistent — eGINs (which use unguarded ∑_{u ∈ V_G}) are the ones requiring TL_2 rather than GTL, and the passage correctly distinguishes them. No actual issue.

This is correct: adding one outer ∑_{x_1} to an expression with free variable x_1 increases summation depth by 1 while staying within 2 indices. However, the passage then says 'So they are 1-MPNNs. Hence, their separation power is bounded by gwl_1^(t)' — but if the readout is in TL_2^(t+1), Theorem 4.4 gives ρ_0(TL_2^(t+1)) relating to gwl_1^(?), and the summary states the clean correspondence at k=1 is ρ_0(TL_2^(t+1)) ⊆ ρ_0(gwl_1^(t)). So gwl_1^(t) is indeed the right bound for a t-layer eGIN with readout. This checks out; no issue.

comments.title: Claim ρ_0(gcr^(t)) = ρ_0(vwl_1^(t)) for eGIN readout

The paper earlier notes (in Section 2) that 1-WL differs from CR in using non-adjacent vertex information, and this distinction 'only matters for vertex embeddings' (citing Grohe 2021). So at the graph level (s=0, after readout), gcr and gwl_1 should indeed coincide in separation power. This claim is consistent with the framework. No issue.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper appears strong and well positioned, offering a conceptually elegant and potentially useful framework for analyzing GNN expressiveness via a tensor language rather than architecture-specific WL arguments. The introduction clearly motivates the need for a general toolbox, situates the contribution relative to prior work, and highlights both recovered known results and new consequences, including bounds for k-IGNs and universality statements. The main strength is the unifying perspective: translating GNN computations into tensor expressions with measurable syntactic properties—number of indices, summation depth, guardedness, and treewidth—seems likely to make expressiveness analysis more systematic and reusable. That said, the beginning is dense and assumes substantial familiarity with WL tests, tensor/query languages, and recent expressiveness literature; readers outside this niche may struggle to distinguish the precise technical novelty from prior MATLANG-based approaches or from Azizian & Lelarge-style universality results. The paper would benefit from an earlier concrete example showing how a standard GNN layer is encoded in the tensor language and how the index/summation-depth bound is read off, since this is central to the claimed usability of the framework. The most significant issue to watch for is whether the tensor-language formalism is genuinely broad enough to capture modern practical GNN components without cumbersome encodings, and whether the resulting WL upper bounds are tight or sometimes overly conservative. Overall, the work seems technically ambitious, well motivated, and likely valuable to the theory of GNN expressiveness, but its impact will depend heavily on the accessibility of the formalism and the sharpness and applicability of the derived bounds.

comments.title: “t rounds are needed” may overstate a sufficient upper-bound argument
comments.quote: _if tensor language expressions use k_ + 1 _indices, then their separation power is bounded by k-_ WL _. Furthermore, if the maximum nesting of summations in the expression is t, then t rounds of k-_ WL _are needed to obtain an upper bound on the separation power._
comments.explanation: The intended high-level message is clear and is consistent with the later formal development: the number of index variables controls the WL dimension, and summation nesting depth controls the number of WL refinement rounds sufficient to bound the expression's separating power. The concern is with the wording “are needed.” Read literally, this suggests a necessity/minimality claim: that an expression of summation depth t cannot in general be bounded using fewer than t WL rounds. That is stronger than the usual syntactic upper-bound statement, and it is not true for arbitrary expressions of depth t, since some summations may be redundant, constant, or otherwise collapsible so that fewer rounds suffice. The phrase is introduced with “Loosely speaking,” which mitigates the problem, but a more precise wording would be “t rounds suffice” or “the bound is given by t rounds.”

comments.title: Ambiguity in “less separating than” for the universality claim
comments.quote: As an example, we show that _k_ -IGNs can approximate any graph function that is less separating than ( _k−_ 1)-WL.
comments.explanation: From the preceding sentence, the intended meaning appears to be functions whose separation power is bounded by, or no greater than, that of (k−1)-WL. That is a standard kind of universality statement in this literature: the model can approximate all invariant/equivariant functions that are constant on the equivalence classes induced by the relevant WL test. However, the phrase “less separating than” is slightly imprecise. It could be read as strictly less separating, excluding functions with exactly the same separating power as (k−1)-WL, or as a comparison between a graph function and an algorithm without explicitly invoking the induced equivalence relation. The context largely resolves the intended meaning, but the claim would be clearer as “whose separating power is bounded by (k−1)-WL.”

comments.title: Strict WL/CR hierarchy statements are overstated for fixed rounds and fixed graph size
comments.quote: The following strict inclusions are known: for all _k ≥_ 1, _ρ_ 1(vwl[(] _k[t]_ +1[)][)] _[ ⊂][ρ]_[1][(][vwl] _k_[(] _[t]_[)][)][ and] _[ ρ]_[1][(][vwl] 1[(] _[t]_[)][)] _[ ⊂][ρ]_[1][(][cr][(] _[t]_[)][)][ (][Otto][,][ 2017][;][ Grohe][,][ 2021][).] It is also known that more rounds ( _t_ ) increase the separation power of these algorithms (F¨urer, 2001).
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear and basically correct at a high level: increasing WL dimension, and in this paper's convention using 1-WL rather than CR for vertex embeddings, gives a finer equivalence relation; likewise, additional refinement rounds can give more distinguishing power. The inclusion direction is also right: more separating power corresponds to a smaller equivalence relation. However, the statement as written appears too strong. At t=0, vwl_k^{(0)} on a diagonal tuple (v,...,v) contains only the atomic type of an all-equal tuple, essentially the vertex label repeated, so ρ_1(vwl_{k+1}^{(0)}) and ρ_1(vwl_k^{(0)}) coincide rather than being strictly included. Similarly, cr^{(0)} and vwl_1^{(0)} both encode just the vertex label, so ρ_1(vwl_1^{(0)}) = ρ_1(cr^{(0)}), not a strict subset. There is also a separate quantification issue: earlier the paper fixes graph size n, whereas standard strict WL hierarchy results such as Otto's are usually formulated over classes of structures of unbounded size, not as strictness for every fixed n and every k. Finally, once refinement stabilizes, additional rounds no longer strictly increase separation power, so the last sentence is only true as a monotonic/general hierarchy claim, not literally for every additional round. This can be fixed by replacing the strict inclusions with non-strict inclusions here, or by adding the precise qualifications under which strictness is meant.

comments.title: The informal “any matrix” interpretation is broader than the later graph semantics
comments.quote: In TL, we do not work with specific matrices or indices ranging over [ _n_ ], but focus instead on expressions applicable to any matrix. ... To give them a semantics, we assign to _E_ a matrix _**A** ∈_ R _[n][×][n]_ , to _Pt_ column vectors _**F**_ : _t ∈_ R _[n][×]_[1] , for _t ∈_ [ _ℓ_ ], and to _x_ 1 an index _i ∈ [ _n_ ].
comments.explanation: The authors' intent seems to be an informal bridge from a concrete matrix computation to a symbolic tensor expression, and in the motivating example A is indeed an adjacency matrix. Still, the wording says the expressions are applicable to “any matrix” and that E is assigned an arbitrary real matrix A ∈ R^{n×n}. The formal semantics immediately afterward is narrower: E is a binary graph predicate interpreted as 1 iff ν(x)ν(y) ∈ E_G and 0 otherwise, while P_s is interpreted from vertex labels. Thus, under the semantics actually used for the separation-power results, E is not an arbitrary matrix-valued input but specifically the adjacency relation of a simple graph. A careful reader could therefore wonder whether TL is meant to support arbitrary real-valued matrix inputs, weighted adjacencies, or normalized adjacency matrices directly, or only graph-derived predicates expressible in the language. The text would be clearer if it said “any adjacency matrix/any graph instance” here, or explicitly distinguished the informal matrix-instance view from the later graph semantics.

comments.title: Triangle-detection example relies on graph-level separation not yet provided by Theorem 4.1
comments.quote: As an example of how to use this result, it is well known that triangles can be detected by 2-WL but not by 1-WL. Thus, in order to design GNNs that can detect triangles, layer definitions in TL3 rather than TL2 should be used.
comments.explanation: The intended intuition is clear and essentially correct: a triangle-counting expression naturally uses three vertex variables, and 1-WL/CR cannot distinguish standard triangle-containing vs triangle-free regular examples. However, the immediately preceding formal statement, Theorem 4.1, is stated for \(\rho_1\), i.e. vertex embeddings with one free index variable. Triangle detection is normally a graph-level property, involving graph embeddings or a global readout. The paper introduces graph-level analogues shortly afterward, so the point is likely justified later, but the word “Thus” makes it sound as if it follows directly from Theorem 4.1 alone. A careful reader could be briefly misled unless the example explicitly mentions a graph-level TL expression/readout or points ahead to the graph-embedding result.

comments.title: Path examples conflate paths with walks and overstate the depth lower bound
comments.quote: As an application of this theorem, to detect the existence of paths of length _t_ , the number of guarded layers in GNNs should account for a representation in GTL(Ω) of summation depth of at least _t_ . ... As a simple example, consider _θ_ ( _x_ 1) =[] _x_ 2  _x_ 3 _[E]_[(] _[x]_[1] _[, x]_[2][)] _[·][E]_[(] _[x]_[2] _[, x]_[3][)][ in][ TL][(] 3[2][)] such that [[θ, v]]_G counts the number of paths of length two starting from _v_ .
comments.explanation: There are two closely related path/walk issues. First, the claim about detecting “paths of length t” is ambiguous: it could mean a rooted property at a specified vertex, a graph-level property after readout, a simple path, or merely a walk. Theorem 4.3 supports a tight CR/GTL correspondence by summation depth, but it does not by itself prove the lower bound “at least t” for this particular property. For some global formulations, shallower degree-type summaries may suffice. Second, the displayed length-two expression \(\sum_{x_2}\sum_{x_3} E(x_1,x_2)E(x_2,x_3)\) counts length-two walks from \(v\), including backtracking walks \(v-u-v\), not simple paths under standard graph terminology. If the intended property is a rooted length-t walk/reachable vertex in the unfolding tree, that should be stated explicitly; if simple paths are intended, distinctness constraints such as \(x_3 \neq x_1\) are needed, and the claimed depth lower bound should be supported by an explicit indistinguishability argument.

comments.title: CR-bound and beyond-CR claims need a guarded/TL scope qualification
comments.quote: ## _**1. The separation power of any vertex embedding**_ GNN _**architecture which is an**_ MPNN _**[(]**[t]_ _**[)] is bounded by the power of** t_ _**rounds of color refinement.**_ ... More generally, any GNN looking to go beyond CR must use non-guarded aggregations.
comments.explanation: The intended claims are standard and correct for ordinary guarded message-passing architectures whose aggregation is over neighbors, and the subsequent GIN/eGIN discussion supports this within the GTL/TL framework. However, the wording is broader than what has been established. Earlier the paper defines kth-order MPNNs via TL_{k+1}(Ω), so a “1-MPNN” in that formal sense can include unguarded all-vertex summations, as the eGIN example illustrates. Such models are bounded by 1-WL rather than by color refinement. Similarly, saying that “any GNN” going beyond CR must use non-guarded aggregations is too broad: models can exceed CR by using higher-order tuple states, structural preprocessing/features, positional encodings, IDs/randomness, or other primitives not simply characterized as non-guarded aggregation. The claims would be safer if restricted to standard/guarded MPNNs or to TL-representable vertex-embedding architectures without additional graph information, e.g. “within this TL/GTL formalism, going beyond CR requires leaving the guarded fragment.”

comments.title: Beyond-k-WL criterion needs TL scope and irreducible treewidth qualification
comments.quote: ## _**4. To go beyond the separation power of** k_ _**-**_ WL _**, it is necessary to use**_ GNN _**s whose layers are represented by expressions of treewidth** > k_ _**.**_ ... For example, to go beyond 1-WL, TL3 representable linear algebra operations should be used.
comments.explanation: The surrounding reasoning is clear: expressions of treewidth at most k can be translated into expressions using k+1 indices, and hence their separation power is bounded by k-WL. Therefore, within the class of TL-representable architectures analyzed by the paper, exceeding k-WL requires that the relevant computation not admit a treewidth-≤k representation. The heading, however, states the necessity without that scope qualification, making it appear to apply to arbitrary GNNs with primitives or preprocessing outside TL, for which expression treewidth may not even be defined. There is also a subtle but important distinction between a particular syntactic layer being written with treewidth > k and the absence of any equivalent lower-treewidth representation. The subsequent phrase “TL3 representable” has the same problem in the k=1 case: every TL2 expression is also representable in TL3 by simply not using the third variable, so TL3-representability alone is not sufficient to go beyond 1-WL. The accurate criterion is something like “not reducible to TL2,” “requiring three variables,” or “treewidth-two TL expressions,” within the TL framework.

comments.title: Clique-count matrix is underspecified; the displayed TL expression counts triangles, not arbitrary cliques
comments.quote: If instead, the layer is of the form _σ_ ( _**C** ·_ _**F** ·_ _**W**_ ) where _Cij_ holds the number of cliques containing the edge _ij_ . Then, in TL(Ω) we get expressions containing[] _x_ 2  _x_ 3 _[E]_[(] _[x]_[1] _[, x]_[2][)] _[·][E]_[(] _[x]_[1] _[, x]_[3][)] _[·][E]_[(] _[x]_[2] _[, x]_[3][)][.]
comments.explanation: The displayed expression makes sense if \(C_{ij}\) is intended to count 3-cliques/triangles containing the edge \(ij\): for output vertex \(x_1\), the omitted feature factor would be something like \(P(x_2)\), and \(\sum_{x_3} E(x_1,x_2)E(x_1,x_3)E(x_2,x_3)\) counts common neighbors of \(x_1\) and \(x_2\). The following sentence, “The variables form a 3-clique,” also suggests this is the intended reading. But the prose says “the number of cliques containing the edge \(ij\),” which in standard graph terminology could mean cliques of arbitrary size, maximal cliques, or cliques of a fixed size larger than three. For \(r\)-cliques containing an edge, the corresponding TL expression would require additional variables and would generally have higher treewidth and a different WL bound than \(\mathrm{wl}^{(2)}_2\). Thus the example is correct for triangle counts, but the definition of \(C\) should explicitly say “triangles” or “3-cliques” to support the stated treewidth-two and two-round 2-WL conclusion.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper appears strong and well positioned, offering a conceptually elegant and potentially useful framework for analyzing GNN expressiveness via a tensor language rather than architecture-specific WL arguments. The introduction clearly motivates the need for a general toolbox, situates the contribution relative to prior work, and highlights both recovered known results and new consequences, including bounds for k-IGNs and universality statements. The main strength is the unifying perspective: translating GNN computations into tensor expressions with measurable syntactic properties—number of indices, summation depth, guardedness, and treewidth—seems likely to make expressiveness analysis more systematic and reusable. That said, the beginning is dense and assumes substantial familiarity with WL tests, tensor/query languages, and recent expressiveness literature; readers outside this niche may struggle to distinguish the precise technical novelty from prior MATLANG-based approaches or from Azizian & Lelarge-style universality results. The paper would benefit from an earlier concrete example showing how a standard GNN layer is encoded in the tensor language and how the index/summation-depth bound is read off, since this is central to the claimed usability of the framework. The most significant issue to watch for is whether the tensor-language formalism is genuinely broad enough to capture modern practical GNN components without cumbersome encodings, and whether the resulting WL upper bounds are tight or sometimes overly conservative. Overall, the work seems technically ambitious, well motivated, and likely valuable to the theory of GNN expressiveness, but its impact will depend heavily on the accessibility of the formalism and the sharpness and applicability of the derived bounds.

comments.title: “t rounds are needed” may overstate a sufficient upper-bound argument
comments.quote: _if tensor language expressions use k_ + 1 _indices, then their separation power is bounded by k-_ WL _. Furthermore, if the maximum nesting of summations in the expression is t, then t rounds of k-_ WL _are needed to obtain an upper bound on the separation power._
comments.explanation: The intended high-level message is clear and is consistent with the later formal development: the number of index variables controls the WL dimension, and summation nesting depth controls the number of WL refinement rounds sufficient to bound the expression's separating power. My concern is only with the wording “are needed.” Read literally, this suggests a necessity/minimality claim: that an expression of summation depth t cannot in general be bounded using fewer than t WL rounds. That is stronger than the usual syntactic upper-bound statement, and it is not true for arbitrary expressions of depth t, since some summations may be redundant, constant, or otherwise collapsible so that fewer rounds suffice. The phrase is introduced with “Loosely speaking,” which mitigates the problem, but a more precise wording would be “t rounds suffice” or “the bound is given by t rounds.”

comments.title: Ambiguity in “less separating than” for the universality claim
comments.quote: As an example, we show that _k_ -IGNs can approximate any graph function that is less separating than ( _k−_ 1)-WL.
comments.explanation: From the preceding sentence, I infer that the authors mean functions whose separation power is bounded by, or no greater than, that of (k−1)-WL. That is a standard kind of universality statement in this literature: the model can approximate all invariant/equivariant functions that are constant on the equivalence classes induced by the relevant WL test. However, the phrase “less separating than” is slightly imprecise. It could be read as strictly less separating, excluding functions with exactly the same separating power as (k−1)-WL, or as a comparison between a graph function and an algorithm without explicitly invoking the induced equivalence relation. The context largely resolves the intended meaning, but the claim would be clearer as “whose separating power is bounded by (k−1)-WL.”

comments.title: Strict WL/CR hierarchy statements are overstated for fixed rounds and fixed graph size
comments.quote: The following strict inclusions are known: for all _k ≥_ 1, _ρ_ 1(vwl[(] _k[t]_ +1[)][)] _[ ⊂][ρ]_[1][(][vwl] _k_[(] _[t]_[)][)][ and] _[ ρ]_[1][(][vwl] 1[(] _[t]_[)][)] _[ ⊂][ρ]_[1][(][cr][(] _[t]_[)][)][ (][Otto][,][ 2017][;][ Grohe][,][ 2021][).] It is also known that more rounds ( _t_ ) increase the separation power of these algorithms (F¨urer, 2001).
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear and basically correct at a high level: increasing the WL dimension, and in this paper's convention using 1-WL rather than CR for vertex embeddings, gives a finer equivalence relation; likewise, additional refinement rounds can give more distinguishing power. The inclusion direction is also right: more separating power corresponds to a smaller equivalence relation. However, the statement as written appears too strong. At t=0, vwl_k^{(0)} on a diagonal tuple (v,...,v) contains only the atomic type of an all-equal tuple, essentially the vertex label repeated, so ρ_1(vwl_{k+1}^{(0)}) and ρ_1(vwl_k^{(0)}) coincide rather than being strictly included. Similarly, cr^{(0)} and vwl_1^{(0)} both encode just the vertex label, so ρ_1(vwl_1^{(0)}) = ρ_1(cr^{(0)}), not a strict subset. There is also a separate quantification issue: earlier the paper fixes graph size n, whereas standard strict WL hierarchy results such as Otto's are usually formulated over classes of structures of unbounded size, not as strictness for every fixed n and every k. Finally, once refinement stabilizes, additional rounds no longer strictly increase separation power, so the last sentence is only true as a monotonic/general hierarchy claim, not literally for every additional round. This can be fixed by replacing the strict inclusions with non-strict inclusions here, or by adding the precise qualifications under which strictness is meant.

comments.title: The informal “any matrix” interpretation is broader than the later graph semantics
comments.quote: In TL, we do not work with specific matrices or indices ranging over [ _n_ ], but focus instead on expressions applicable to any matrix. ... To give them a semantics, we assign to _E_ a matrix _**A** ∈_ R _[n][×][n]_ , to _Pt_ column vectors _**F**_ : _t ∈_ R _[n][×]_[1] , for _t ∈_ [ _ℓ_ ], and to _x_ 1 an index _i ∈_ [ _n_ ].
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent here as an informal bridge from a concrete matrix computation to a symbolic tensor expression, and in the motivating example A is indeed an adjacency matrix. Still, the wording says the expressions are applicable to “any matrix” and that E is assigned an arbitrary real matrix A ∈ R^{n×n}. The formal semantics immediately afterward is narrower: E is a binary graph predicate interpreted as 1 iff ν(x)ν(y) ∈ E_G and 0 otherwise, while P_s is interpreted from vertex labels. Thus, under the semantics actually used for the separation-power results, E is not an arbitrary matrix-valued input but specifically the adjacency relation of a simple graph. A careful reader could therefore wonder whether TL is meant to support arbitrary real-valued matrix inputs, weighted adjacencies, or normalized adjacency matrices directly, or only graph-derived predicates expressible in the language. The surrounding context mostly resolves this for the example, but the text would be clearer if it said “any adjacency matrix/any graph instance” here, or explicitly distinguished the informal matrix-instance view from the later graph semantics.

comments.title: Triangle-detection example relies on graph-level separation not yet provided by Theorem 4.1
comments.quote: As an example of how to use this result, it is well known that triangles can be detected by 2-WL but not by 1-WL. Thus, in order to design GNNs that can detect triangles, layer definitions in TL3 rather than TL2 should be used.
comments.explanation: The intended intuition is clear and essentially correct: a triangle-counting expression naturally uses three vertex variables, and 1-WL/CR cannot distinguish standard triangle-containing vs triangle-free regular examples. However, I checked the immediately preceding formal statement and Theorem 4.1 is stated for \(\rho_1\), i.e. vertex embeddings with one free index variable. Triangle detection is normally a graph-level property, involving graph embeddings or a global readout. The paper does introduce graph-level analogues shortly afterward, so the point is likely justified later, but the word "Thus" makes it sound as if it follows directly from Theorem 4.1 alone. A careful reader could be briefly misled unless the example explicitly mentions a graph-level TL expression/readout or points ahead to the graph-embedding result.

comments.title: Claim about paths of length \(t\) is ambiguous and not justified by the stated theorem alone
comments.quote: As an application of this theorem, to detect the existence of paths of length _t_ , the number of guarded layers in GNNs should account for a representation in GTL(Ω) of summation depth of at least _t_ .
comments.explanation: I understand the intended message: guarded summations propagate information one edge at a time, so detecting a length-\(t\) neighborhood phenomenon should require \(t\) rounds/layers. Theorem 4.3 also supports a tight CR/GTL correspondence by summation depth. What remains unclear is the exact property being claimed. "Existence of paths of length \(t\)" could mean a rooted property at a specified vertex, a graph-level property after readout, a simple path, or merely a walk. The theorem equates separation power with \(t\)-round CR; it does not by itself prove the lower bound "at least \(t\)" for this particular property, and for global path existence some cases can be detected via shallower degree-type summaries depending on the formulation. If the intended property is rooted existence of a length-\(t\) walk/reachable vertex in the unfolding tree, this should be stated more precisely, ideally with the standard indistinguishability argument showing that depth \(<t\) cannot suffice.

comments.title: Length-two example counts walks, not simple paths
comments.quote: As a simple example, consider _θ_ ( _x_ 1) =[] _x_ 2  _x_ 3 _[E]_[(] _[x]_[1] _[, x]_[2][)] _[·][E]_[(] _[x]_[2] _[, x]_[3][)][ in][ TL][(] 3[2][)][such that][ [[] _[θ, v]_[]]] _[G]_[ counts] the number of paths of length two starting from _v_ .
comments.explanation: The algebraic point of the example is sound: the expression can be reassociated/reindexed to use only two variables, illustrating the treewidth-based reduction. However, the stated combinatorial interpretation is not quite right under standard graph-theoretic terminology. The formula \(\sum_{x_2}\sum_{x_3} E(x_1,x_2)E(x_2,x_3)\) counts length-two walks from \(v\), including backtracking walks \(v-u-v\). For instance, in a single-edge graph it counts one length-two walk from each endpoint, although there is no simple path of length two. If the authors mean walks, replacing "paths" by "walks" would fix the issue; if they mean simple paths, an additional distinctness condition such as \(x_3\neq x_1\) is needed, with the corresponding reduced expression adjusted accordingly.

comments.title: Scope ambiguity in the CR bound for “MPNN” versus “1-MPNN”
comments.quote: ## _**1. The separation power of any vertex embedding**_ GNN _**architecture which is an**_ MPNN _**[(]**[t]_ _**[)] is bounded by the power of** t_ _**rounds of color refinement.**_
comments.explanation: The intended claim is standard and correct for ordinary message-passing architectures whose aggregation is over neighbors, and the subsequent GIN example supports this by explicitly representing the layers in the guarded tensor language GTL. What initially gave me pause is that earlier the paper defines kth-order MPNNs via TL_{k+1}(Ω), so a “1-MPNN” in that formal sense can include unguarded all-vertex summations, as the very next eGIN example illustrates. Such models are bounded by 1-WL rather than by color refinement. Thus the statement is fine if “MPNN” here means standard/guarded message passing, but it could be read as applying to all 1-MPNNs in the paper’s TL sense. A small qualifier such as “standard/guarded MPNN” or “GTL-representable MPNN” would remove the ambiguity.

comments.title: Overstated necessity of non-guarded aggregation for exceeding CR
comments.quote: More generally, any GNN looking to go beyond CR must use non-guarded aggregations.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: within the authors’ tensor-language framework, GTL captures guarded neighbor-based aggregation and is bounded by color refinement, while eGIN exceeds this guarded fragment by adding an unguarded global summation. In that setting, leaving the guarded fragment is indeed necessary to exceed the CR bound. However, as written the sentence quantifies over “any GNN” and identifies “non-guarded aggregations” as the necessary mechanism. That is broader than what has been established locally: a model might go beyond CR by using higher-order tuple states, structural preprocessing/features, positional encodings, IDs/randomness, or other primitives not simply characterized as non-guarded aggregation. The claim would be safer if restricted to TL-representable vertex-embedding architectures without additional graph information, or phrased as “within this TL/GTL formalism, going beyond CR requires leaving the guarded fragment.”

comments.title: Treewidth condition stated as an unrestricted necessity
comments.quote: ## _**4. To go beyond the separation power of** k_ _**-**_ WL _**, it is necessary to use**_ GNN _**s whose layers are represented by expressions of treewidth** > k_ _**.**_
comments.explanation: The surrounding discussion makes the intended reasoning clear: expressions of treewidth at most k can be translated into expressions using k+1 indices, and hence their separation power is bounded by k-WL. Therefore, within the class of TL-representable architectures analyzed by this paper, exceeding k-WL requires that the relevant computation not admit a treewidth-≤k representation. The heading, however, states the necessity without that scope qualification. Taken literally, it seems to apply to arbitrary GNNs, including architectures with primitives or preprocessing outside TL, for which the expression-treewidth criterion may not even be defined. There is also a subtle distinction between a particular syntactic layer being written with treewidth > k and the absence of any equivalent lower-treewidth representation. The statement is likely correct in the intended TL-normal-form sense, but the heading overstates it unless the framework restriction is made explicit.

comments.title: “TL3 representable” is too weak as a criterion for going beyond 1-WL
comments.quote: For example, to go beyond 1-WL, TL3 representable linear algebra operations should be used.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point from the surrounding discussion: by the paper’s index/treewidth results, expressions requiring three variables, equivalently treewidth two in the relevant sense, are the first place where one can hope to exceed the 1-WL/CR bound. The subsequent examples correctly contrast a path-shaped expression of treewidth one with a triangle-shaped expression of treewidth two. However, as written, “TL3 representable” is not sufficient to express this criterion, because every TL2 expression is also representable in TL3 by simply not using the third variable. Thus a TL3-representable operation may still be only treewidth one and bounded by 1-WL/CR, as the immediately following \(\mathbf A^3\) example illustrates. The statement would be clearer and technically accurate if it said something like “operations not reducible to TL2,” “operations requiring three variables,” or “treewidth-two TL expressions.”
